content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
The Honorable Jerry Eaves 
Member of the Assembly 
state Capitol 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Eaves: 
April 14, 1989 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 
I-89-~ 
You have requested advice regarding application of the mass 
mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act,,)l/, as 
amended by Proposition 73 on the June 7, 1988 ballot. 
QUESTION 
May you send out at public expense several thousand postcards 
announcing two "seniorfairs" at which you will speak and which you 
are sponsoring for seniors? 
CONCLUSION 
A mass mailing of the postcards may not be sent at public 
expense so long as the text of the postcards makes reference to 
you. 
If the text does not make reference to you, the mailing may 
include your name in the return address if it is sent on your 
standard letterhead, forms or envelopes. 
FACTS 
You have asked whether you may send a postcard at public 
expense to several thousand constituents. 
The postcard announces 
two separate events which you are sponsoring for seniors. 
You 
will be present at both events to talk to seniors and to let them 
know about your legislative program. 
II Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations 
section 18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804,0807 • (916) 322,5660 


The Honorable Jerry Eaves 
April 14, 1989 
Page 2 
ANALYSIS 
section 89001 provides that no mass mailing shall be sent at 
public expense. 
The term "mass mailing" means over two hundred 
substantially similar pieces of mail. 
(Section 82041.5.) 
Regulation 18901 (copy enclosed) clarifies that the purpose 
of Section 89001 is to prevent elected officers from using public 
funds to send out newsletters and other mass mailings. 
Specifically, a mass mailing may not be sent at public expense if 
it is sent by an elected officer and includes the name, office, 
photograph or other reference to the elected officer on the 
document. 
(Regulation 18901 (c) (2) (A) (ii).) 
Subdivision (f) of 
the regulation lists several exemptions from the prohibition, two 
of which are relevant here. 
Subdivision (f) (8) exempts: 
(8) 
Mailings sent to the elected officer's 
constituents which directly relate to that elected 
official's incumbent governmental duties and which 
solely include the time, date, place, and a concise 
description of the subject matter of a public meeting to 
be held by the elected officer; 
Subdivision (f) (10) exempts: 
(10) 
Announcements of the time, date, place, and 
subject matter of official agency events which include a 
listing of elected officers and others who will 
participate in the events. 
The postcard you have sent us (copy attached) includes your 
name in its text. 
Accordingly, unless it falls under one of the 
listed exemptions, it may not be sent in a mass mailing at public 
expense. 
We do not believe the postcard falls under either of the 
exemptions. 
The exemptions are to be narrowly construed. 
(Lancaster Advice Letter, No. I-89-026; Clark Advice Letter, No. 
I-89-031; Kurle Advice Letter, No. A-89-099; copies enclosed.) 
The exemption in subdivision (f) (8) applies to notices of a public 
meeting to be held by the elected officer. 
The postcard notifies 
constituents about the "seniorfairs" at which "more than 70 
experts" will be available to answer questions on various issues 
of interest to seniors. 
The seniorfairs will be held on two 
different days from 10:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m. 
The postcard then 
mentions that you will also be there to talk about your 
legislative program, and issues of concern to the Legislature. 
This is not the type of notice to which the exemption applies. 
The "seniorfairs" are much more than public meetings at which you 
will enter into an exchange with your constituents relating to 
your incumbent governmental duties. 
They are informational events 


The Honorable Jerry Eaves 
April 14, 1989 
Page 3 
at which seniors will be able to obtain information about issues 
from a variety of individuals and sources. 
For example, the 
notice provides that n[M]ore than 70 experts will be available to 
answer your questions on issues such as catastrophic health laws, 
crime prevention, campaign programs, government benefits, fitness, 
free health screenings, and much more. n 
The postcard also fails to qualify as an announcement of an 
official agency event as described in subdivision (f) (10). 
This 
event is not sponsored by the Legislature. 
Furthermore, the 
postcard contains information beyond that allowed under the 
exemption. 
The postcards may be sent at public expense if there is no 
reference made to you in the text. 
Your name may appear in the 
return address if the mailing is sent on your standard letterhead, 
forms or envelopes. 
(Mott-Smith Advice Letter, No. A-89-153i 
Gentry Advice Letter No. A-89-166i copies enclosed.) 
I trust this answers your question. 
If you have any further 
questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
ener~ ~~~l~ 
John G. McLean 
Co nsel, Legal Division 
DMG:JGM:ld 
Enclosure 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
March 31, 1989 
Honorable Jerry Eaves 
Assemblymember, Sixty-Sixth District 
state Capitol 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Re: 
Letter No. 89-189 
Dear Assemblyman Eaves: 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act 
was received on March 30, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices 
commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice request, 
you may contact John McLean an attorney in the Legal Division, 
directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or 
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21 
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. 
If more 
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response 
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to 
information needed. 
If your request is for informal assistance, 
we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission 
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
"O . &-.. ~ }/"h 
( L, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804,0807 • (916)322,5660 


SACRAMENTO OFFICE 
State Capitol 
Sacramento 
California 95814 
(916) 445-4843 
DISTRICT OFFICE 
224 
Riverside Avenue 
Suite A 
California 92376 
820-1902 
Mr. John H. Larson 
Chairman 
sstmhln 
tgisiaturt 
ASSISTANT MAJORITY FLOOR LEADER 
ASSEMBLYMAN, SIXTY-SIXTH DISTRICT 
REPRESENT'ING THE 
AND 
OF RIALTO, 
COLTON, ONTARIO, BLOOMINGTON, AND LAKE ARROWHEAD 
March 30, 1989 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
Sacrament ,CA 
95814 
Dear Mr~"'"U""'-
n: 
COMMITTEES: 
FINANCE AND INSURANCE 
CHAIR~!AN, UNEMPLOY\lENl 
AND DISABILiTY 
SUBCOMMITTEE 
TRANSPORTA TlON 
CHAfRMAN, CAUFORNIA 
HIGHWAY PATROL 
SUBCOMMITTEE 
AND COMMERCE 
WAYS 
CHAIRMAN, SELECT 
COMMITTEE ON AViATION 
Th~ is to request an FPPC opinion as to the legality of 
mailin 
a postcard at government expense to several thousand 
constituents. 
The postcard announces two separate events which I 
am sponsoring for seniors and will be attended by experts on 
issues affecting the elderly. 
I will be present at 
both events to talk to seniors about the issues which are 
important to them and to let them know about my legislative 
program. 
The text of the postcard in enclosed. 
In order to meet time-line requirements, I would like your 
reply within 10 working days. 
Sincerely, 
JE:deb 
Enclosure 


1'1AF~ 30 '89 12: 31 EAVES 
Celebrate Older Americans ~bnth 
at the second Annual seniorfairs! 
!'Pre than 70 experts will be avai1abl~ t.o an~r your questi.ons 
on issues such as catastrophic health laws/ crime prevention, 
companion programs, government benefits, fitness, free health 
screenings, and much more. 
Assemblyman Jerry Eaves will be there to talk with you about his 
legislative program, issues of ooncern to the Legislature and to 
answer any questions you may have about these issues. 
Learn what servioes are available for yoUl 
Friday, May 12th, 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. 
peter LMque Cent.er, 292 "011 street, 0':>1 ton

announcing two "seniorfairs" at which you will speak and which you 
are sponsoring for seniors?

CONCLUSION: A mass mailing of the postcards may not be sent at public 
expense so long as the text of the postcards makes reference to 
you. 
If the text does not make reference to you, the mailing may 
include your name in the return address if it is sent on your 
standard letterhead, forms or envelopes.
